displayTitle: Every Bad Film Has a Cause — and a Cure
kicker: RT LEVEL II // CAUSES AND CURES

beats.narration: Hang a film on the viewer — something's wrong. Retakes cost money, dose, and schedule, so a Level two names the cause and the cure.

payload.title: BAD RADIOGRAPH
payload.sub: Name the cause. State the cure.

beats.narration: Almost every bad film traces to one of three bins: exposure, processing, or handling and storage. Sort the symptom first.

payload.heading: Three bins hold almost every cause

beats.narration: Density first. Too dark: too much exposure, or developer run hot. Too light? Mirror the list — iridium one ninety-two halves about every seventy-four days.

payload.heading: Density: too dark, too light
payload.note: IR-192: HALF STRENGTH ABOUT EVERY 74 DAYS

beats.narration: Artifacts are false images. Crimp before exposure prints light crescents; after, dark. Static draws dark branching lines; air bells in the developer leave light spots.

payload.heading: Artifacts: learn the signatures
payload.note: FALSE IMAGES MIMIC — OR HIDE — REAL FLAWS

beats.narration: So the professional habit: identify every mark first, and never call a discontinuity until you have ruled out the artifact.

payload.sub: Rule out the artifact before you call the flaw.
